overview: Dimir Control is a Standard archetype that combines blue's value and answers with black's harsh removal and punishment. The deck aims to stabilize the board, exhaust the opponent's resources, and seize control through responsive play and card advantage. It relies on versatile Omen dragons and flexible sideboard options to navigate a creature-heavy meta.

keep_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one turn-1 land drop, and early interaction (counters, spot removal, or cantrips).
Keep hands that can play a land and a spell on turn 1, or two lands and a cantrip.
mulligan_criteria: Can't do anything for quite a time
Lacks colored mana sources early on against fast decks
Mulligan to hands that can stabilize against Izzet spells or Landfall ramp, typically requiring at least two lands and a way to interact or draw cards.

examples.reason: It has land drops, decent answers, and can create enough card advantage.
Great in some situations but dangerous in others; you'll have to rely on Demolition Field if you can't find other colors.
It can't do anything for quite a time, and this is dangerous.

early_game: Stabilize the board and use removals to counter the opponent's main strategy
Rely on Demolition Field if the mana base lacks colored sources
Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap interaction if available.
Turn 2: Develop mana, cast a mid-cost threat or hold interaction for key Izzet spells/Landfall triggers.
Prioritize playing lands to ensure consistent mana for both colors.
If opening with a hand that has multiple lands and no early spells, consider a mulligan unless you have a strong plan to stabilize.
mid_game: Be responsive instead of proactive at all times
Don't let the opponent build a solid board or create excessive value
Use life points as a resource against aggressive decks
Balance mana between interaction and development.
Never overextend into known removal or board wipes.
Use cantrips to maintain hand size and dig for answers.
Track graveyard usage, as Landfall and Izzet decks often rely on it for value.
Commit threats only when you have card advantage or can answer their answers.
Hold back interaction until you see if the opponent is going for a combo or a wide board.
late_game: Lean into removals and card advantage until you have enough mana to play threats
Put Ugin in play at the right time to potentially win the game
Make the opponent exhaust their resources until the game is under your control
Leverage superior late-game threats like Perigee Beckoner or value engines that out-value the opponent's remaining resources.
Win by out-drawing them, removing their remaining answers, and deploying a threat they cannot interact with.
Go all-in when you have established card advantage, have answered or out-valued their interaction, and the board state allows you to push damage or deploy a finisher without fear of immediate wipe.
Also commit when the opponent is low on resources and you can close the game in 1-2 turns.

key_cards.name: Scavenger Regent
key_cards.notes: Has the Omen ability to cast as an instant/sorcery and shuffle back, or as a creature.

key_cards.name: Marang River Regent
key_cards.notes: Creates card advantage whenever needed and shares the versatile Omen ability.

key_cards.name: Dispelling Exhale
key_cards.notes: Used as much as possible to stop opponent spells and stabilize.

key_cards.name: Consult the Star Charts
key_cards.notes: Functions like Impulse early on, and with Kicker turns into a Memory Deluge later.

key_cards.name: Demolition Field
key_cards.notes: Crucial fallback when opening hands lack sufficient colored mana sources.

key_cards.name: Ugin
key_cards.notes: Incredible in matchups like Esper Bounce; can win the game if played at the right time.

key_cards.name: Tishana's Tidebinder
key_cards.notes: Very versatile and fits many matchups.

key_cards.name: Preacher
key_cards.notes: Performs well when you need extra resources.

key_cards.name: Liliana
key_cards.notes: Serves as an alternative win condition.

sequencing_tips: Try to be responsive instead of proactive at all times
Don't let the opponent build a solid board
Use your life points as a resource as best as you can
Avoid hands that are too greedy or too slow
Put Ugin in play at the right time to maximize impact
Common mistakes include overplaying interaction on low-impact threats, failing to track the opponent's graveyard, and mulliganing too aggressively.
Always prioritize card advantage in control matchups, and never assume a threat is dead if you have a way to answer their answer.
Practice recognizing when to pivot from control to aggressive value based on the board state.

matchups.advice: Deal with the board well and prevent the opponent from creating value. Prioritize removals that exile cards to directly counter their strategy.
Be responsive instead of proactive. Win through the right sequence of cards and trades without letting them establish a board.
Use life points as a resource and manage your answers carefully. Know how to handle their main threats correctly.
Pay attention to their potential control pivot. Use removals efficiently to keep key permanents off the board.
Prevent them from playing creatures freely. Lean into removals and card advantage until you can deploy threats like Ugin at the optimal moment.

Use split spells, multi-target effects, or discard to remove counterspells before they can be cast. Deploy uncounterable threats or value engines that win even if countered. Force them to use multiple counterspells by threatening multiple lines of play.

Apply early pressure with spot removal and discard to disrupt their mana development. Use board wipes to reset their board when they commit to a wide or high-value board. Prioritize removing their key ramp sources before they can cast their finishers.

Stabilize with cheap counters and spot removal. Dig for card advantage to outlast their burst. Do not overcommit to the board; instead, trade efficiently and win the resource war. Bring in additional removal and discard post-sideboard.

Apply consistent pressure with mid-game threats and card advantage. Force them to use interaction on your threats rather than their own game plan. Sideboard in hate cards that disrupt their combo or control shell. Win by out-valuing them in the late game.

Card advantage wins the mirror. Hold up interaction to punish overextensions. Win the resource war by drawing more cards and deploying threats that force unfavorable trades. Sideboard in additional card draw and late-game finishers to outlast them.

common_threats: Watch for mana bases that indicate combo or control plans.
Note when opponents hold up mana for counterspells (typically 2+ mana open on their turn).
Track graveyard accumulation for Landfall triggers or flashback/recurse engines.
Be alert to token generation or pump spells that signal an aggressive push.

overview_evidence.evidence_span: The idea behind this deck, like any Control strategy, is to stabilize the board and make the opponent exhaust their resources until they can't do anything else and the game is under your control.

win_conditions_evidence.evidence_span: Scavenger Regent and Marang River Regent show what this deck can do and should do really well... main win conditions.
Liliana can be an alternative win condition

sequencing_tips_evidence.evidence_span: Try to be responsive instead of proactive at all times, and don't let them build a solid board.
You must also use your life points as a resource as best as you can.
avoid hands that are too greedy or too slow.

common_threats_evidence.evidence_span: centers around Mosswood Dreadknight above all else
don't let Ral, Crackling Wit stay on the board. It is an incredible source of value and almost as dangerous as Vivi Ornitier
Their Otter tokens can destroy your life points very quickly
